schools.summary: Gill: the apostle, having discoursed of the believer's inward struggle (Romans 7), now proceeds to the solid ground of spiritual peace and joy, arising from justification and adoption, the purposes of God, and the unchangeable love of God; no condemnation to those in Christ Jesus — the triumphant conclusion of the doctrine of justification (the complete, forever freedom from condemnation, grounded in union with Christ and his imputed righteousness, the believer in Christ sharing his righteousness); the walking after the Spirit the mark/evidence of those in Christ, not the ground of the freedom (Christ's righteousness).

debates.question: Is the walk after the Spirit the ground or the evidence of no condemnation?

sides.position: The evidence/mark of those in Christ, not the ground — the freedom is grounded in Christ's imputed righteousness, the walk describing the justified

themes: no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus
walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit
union with Christ the ground
the walk the mark, not the ground

schools.summary: Gill: the words of difficult interpretation — 'the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus' may mean the gospel, or the principle of grace and life wrought by the Spirit; 'the law of sin and death' the law of Moses (which condemns) or the principle of indwelling sin; the chief sense — the new governing principle of spiritual life imparted by the Spirit (the reign of grace and life in Christ) freeing the believer from the ruling power of indwelling sin (which works death) — the liberating power of the Spirit (2 Corinthians 3:17), the new principle conquering the old, grounding the no condemnation (v.1).

debates.question: What is 'the law of the Spirit of life' and 'the law of sin and death'?

sides.position: The new governing principle of spiritual life by the Spirit, freeing from the ruling power of indwelling sin (the chief sense)
The gospel freeing from the condemning law of Moses

themes: the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus
made me free from the law of sin and death
the liberating power of the Spirit
the new principle conquering the old

schools.summary: Gill: the law could not justify or give life, being weakened not in itself but through the flesh (the corrupt nature that cannot fulfil it); what the law could not do, God did — sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh (true, real flesh yet without sin, the careful phrase guarding both the reality of the incarnation and the sinlessness, Hebrews 4:15) and 'for sin' (as a sacrifice/sin-offering), condemning sin in the flesh of Christ (executing the judgment due to sin upon the Son as the sin-bearer, the substitution, the atonement, the sin punished in Christ so the believer is freed).

themes: what the law could not do... weak through the flesh
his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh
condemned sin in the flesh
the incarnation and atonement

schools.summary: Gill: by 'the righteousness of the law' the moral law's requirement (holiness of nature, righteousness of life, the active righteousness), and by Christ's obedience to it we are made righteous; the purpose of the atonement (v.3) that the law's righteousness might be fulfilled in believers — chiefly by Christ's obedience imputed (Christ as their representative perfectly keeping the law, his righteousness reckoned, the law's demand met 'in us'/in our Head), evidenced by the Spirit-led walk (the imputed righteousness and the Spirit-wrought obedience); the law honoured (not set aside but established, Romans 3:31), the distinction of imputation (ground) and sanctification (evidence).

debates.question: How is 'the righteousness of the law fulfilled in us'?

sides.position: Chiefly by Christ's obedience imputed (the law's demand met in our Head), evidenced by the Spirit-led walk

themes: the righteousness of the law might be fulfilled in us
walk... after the Spirit
Christ's imputed obedience
the law honoured

schools.summary: Gill: by 'flesh' the corruption of nature, to be 'after the flesh' not merely to have flesh (the best saints have it, regenerating grace not removing it) but to be governed by it (those in whom corrupt nature is the ruling principle); the two classes — those after the flesh minding the things of the flesh (the carnal, sensual, worldly pursuits of the unregenerate), those after the Spirit the things of the Spirit (the spiritual, heavenly things of the regenerate) — the governing principle determining the bent of the mind (the affections following the ruling nature, Matthew 6:21), the distinction of having flesh and being after it, the minding the test of the class.

themes: they that are after the flesh mind the things of the flesh
they that are after the Spirit the things of the Spirit
the governing principle
the minding revealing the man

schools.summary: Gill: the phrase includes the best part of corrupt man (the mind, understanding, will, affections, thoughts), may be rendered 'the wisdom of the flesh,' called so to distinguish it from the wisdom from above (the wisest part of man but carnal); to be carnally minded is death (spiritual death now, alienation from God, issuing in eternal death) but to be spiritually minded is life and peace (spiritual and eternal life, the peace of reconciliation and a quiet conscience) — the two minds issuing in the two opposite ends (the fruit of each bent), even the wisdom of the flesh ending in death.

themes: to be carnally minded is death
to be spiritually minded is life and peace
the two opposite ends
the fruit of each bent

schools.summary: Gill: the carnal mind not only an enemy but enmity itself against God — against his being (it wishes he were not), his perfections, purposes, providences, law, and gospel; the carnal mind enmity against God (not merely at enmity but enmity itself, the very nature opposed, the deep ingrained hostility), not subject to the law of God (the insubordination, the rebellion) 'neither indeed can be' (the utter inability, the depravity so deep the unrenewed nature cannot submit, the total inability, 1 Corinthians 2:14) — the depth of depravity exposed, the necessity of the new nature (regeneration).

themes: the carnal mind is enmity against God
not subject to the law of God, neither indeed can be
the radical opposition to God
the utter inability of the unrenewed nature

schools.summary: Barnes: 'they that are in the flesh' are unrenewed sinners, following supremely the desires of the flesh, not led by the Spirit, and while thus in the flesh cannot please God; Gill: the same as those after the flesh; the unregenerate (governed by corrupt nature) cannot please God — being enmity against him and unable to submit to his law (v.7), nothing done in enmity, without faith, from a carnal heart can please him (Hebrews 11:6, 'without faith it is impossible to please him'), the impossibility (not merely do not but cannot); the necessity of the new nature (the Spirit) for acceptable service, the believer (in the Spirit, v.9) by contrast pleasing God.

themes: they that are in the flesh cannot please God
the unregenerate unable to please him
being enmity and unable to submit
the necessity of the new nature

schools.summary: Barnes: 'ye' who are Christians are the opposite of those described (v.8) — not under the full influence of corrupt desires but spiritually minded, under the direction of the Holy Spirit; Gill: not in a state of unregeneracy but of grace; believers not in the flesh but in the Spirit if the Spirit of God dwell in them, and anyone who has not the Spirit of Christ is none of his — the indwelling Spirit the decisive mark of belonging to Christ (the test of the true believer, not profession or outward religion but the Spirit dwelling, without whom none is his); the same Spirit called 'of God' and 'of Christ' (the deity of the Spirit and of Christ implied).

themes: ye are not in the flesh, but in the Spirit
if any man have not the Spirit of Christ, he is none of his
the indwelling Spirit the mark
the decisive test of belonging to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is in his people not as in the world generally but formed in their hearts by the Spirit in regeneration, dwelling as his own; if Christ be in the believer, the body is dead because of sin (mortal, appointed to die for sin, the mortality remaining) but the Spirit is life because of righteousness — the chief sense the believer's renewed spirit alive (with spiritual and eternal life) because of the imputed righteousness of Christ (some taking 'the Spirit' as the Holy Spirit, the life-giver); the body's death not final (to be quickened, v.11, the resurrection hope), the spirit's life grounded in the imputed righteousness.

debates.question: Does 'the Spirit is life' mean the believer's renewed spirit or the Holy Spirit?

sides.position: Chiefly the believer's renewed spirit, alive (spiritual/eternal life) because of the imputed righteousness
The Holy Spirit, the life-giver in the believer

themes: if Christ be in you, the body is dead because of sin
the Spirit is life because of righteousness
the mortal body and the living spirit
life by imputed righteousness

schools.summary: Gill: this refers not to the continued work of sanctification but to the future quickening of the mortal body (the resurrection), for it is the mortal bodies, not the souls, that are said to be quickened; if the Spirit of the God who raised Jesus dwells in the believer, God will also quicken (raise) his mortal body by that indwelling Spirit — the resurrection of the body the certain consequence of the indwelling Spirit (the same power that raised Christ raising the believer, 1 Corinthians 15:23), the indwelling Spirit the pledge and agent of the resurrection (the earnest, the guarantee, the hope of the body, the redemption of the body, v.23).

debates.question: Does 'quicken your mortal bodies' mean sanctification or the bodily resurrection?

sides.position: The future bodily resurrection (the mortal bodies, not the souls, quickened) — not the continued work of sanctification

themes: the Spirit of him that raised up Jesus
quicken your mortal bodies by his Spirit
the resurrection of the body
the indwelling Spirit the pledge

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having shown the great reward of the spiritual life (Christ dwelling, the body quickened, the way of virtue made easier), Paul fitly introduces an exhortation — yet not merely 'we ought not to live after the flesh' but in a more striking way 'we are debtors,' and saying 'we are debtors not to the flesh' indicates we are debtors to the Spirit; the believer owes nothing to the flesh (which has done him no good, only brought death, v.13) but owes all to the Spirit (the giver of life) — the obligation of grace, the debt of love and gratitude to the Spirit, the motive for holiness.

themes: we are debtors, not to the flesh
debtors to the Spirit (implied)
owing nothing to the flesh
the obligation of grace

schools.summary: Gill: to live after the flesh, in a continued course of sinning, is contrary to the grace of God; though flesh remains in the believer, it has not the dominion, and the believer mortifies its deeds through the Spirit; the two ends — living after the flesh ends in death (the carnal life fatal), mortifying the deeds of the body through the Spirit ends in life — the necessity and means of mortification (sin put to death not by mere resolution but through the Spirit's power, Colossians 3:5), the believer's act and the Spirit's power (the duty and the grace), the killing of sin the path and evidence of life.

themes: if ye live after the flesh, ye shall die
through the Spirit... mortify the deeds of the body, ye shall live
the necessity of mortification
sin put to death through the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: the Spirit leads them from sin, from a dependence on their own righteousness, to Christ, in the paths of faith and truth; the leading supposes life and some strength (though much weakness) and is a display of efficacious grace, always for their good (the gentle leading, as a child taught to go, the powerful yet tender guidance); as many as are led by the Spirit of God are the sons of God — the Spirit's leading the mark/evidence of sonship (the led the sons, the leading the proof of the adoption), the comfort of the leading (the believer conscious of being drawn from sin, to Christ, in holiness, assured of his sonship).

themes: as many as are led by the Spirit of God
they are the sons of God
the leading the mark of sonship
the evidence of adoption

schools.summary: Gill: the 'spirit of bondage' not the Holy Spirit (who is a free Spirit, a Spirit of liberty) but the bondage and fear under the law and conviction, the Spirit the believer receives the Spirit of adoption, removing fear by discoveries of love; believers have not received the spirit of bondage again to fear but the Spirit of adoption, by whom they cry 'Abba, Father' (Abba the Aramaic, Father the Greek, the cry of a child, perhaps embracing Jew and Gentile) — the deliverance from slavish fear (the dread of God as judge under the law) and the gift of the filial spirit (the love of God as a Father, 1 John 4:18), the privilege of adoption.

themes: not received the spirit of bondage again to fear
the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father
the filial spirit
deliverance from slavish fear

schools.summary: Barnes: the Holy Spirit is meant (it is of the Spirit the apostle is treating, and it would be forced to say of the temper of adoption that it bore witness); the Spirit testifies, gives evidence, with our spirit (to our minds) that we are children of God — the double witness (the Holy Spirit and the believer's own renewed spirit/conscience together testifying to the adoption), the inward assurance of sonship (the highest comfort, the Spirit witnessing by working the filial disposition, his fruits, enabling the cry 'Abba, Father'), guarded against presumption (the witness joined with the evidence of grace, not a bare impulse).

themes: the Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit
that we are the children of God
the double witness
the inward assurance of sonship

schools.summary: Gill: children (natural or adopted) are heirs to their parents, the believers being children of God are heirs — and according to the dignity of the inheritance, heirs of God himself and joint-heirs with Christ; if children, then heirs — heirs of God (God himself the inheritance, the portion, the all, Lamentations 3:24, not merely created blessings) and joint-heirs with Christ (sharing his inheritance, glory, kingdom) — the surpassing dignity of the heirship, and the path to glory through suffering with Christ (the cross before the crown, 2 Timothy 2:12, the suffering the appointed way not the meritorious cause), the suffering and glory joined.

themes: if children, then heirs
heirs of God, and joint-heirs with Christ
if we suffer with him... glorified together
the path to glory through suffering

schools.summary: Gill: 'this present time' may mean the then-present suffering age or the present time of life, the saints suffering much yet grace and strength answerable to their trials being given; Paul reckoning (the calculation of faith) that the sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory which shall be revealed in us — the present afflictions, however great, infinitely outweighed by the coming glory (the no-comparison, 2 Corinthians 4:17, the light momentary suffering against the eternal weight of glory), the glory revealed 'in us' (the believers themselves glorified), the comfort under suffering.

themes: the sufferings of this present time
not worthy to be compared with the glory
the glory which shall be revealed in us
the calculation of faith

schools.summary: The expositors divide on 'the creature/creation' — Gill takes it (here and v.20-22) chiefly of the Gentile world, longing and groaning for deliverance and the influx of converts; Barnes and most take it of the natural, irrational creation, personified (the word a picture of watching with outstretched neck) as eagerly waiting for the redemption; the creation, subjected to the curse (Genesis 3:17), longing for the manifestation (revealing in glory) of the sons of God — the creation's deliverance linked to the sons' glorification (v.21, the destinies joined, the world restored with the sons).

sides.position: The whole natural, irrational creation, personified as waiting for the redemption (the dominant view)
Chiefly the Gentile world, longing for deliverance and the influx of converts

themes: the earnest expectation of the creature
waiteth for the manifestation of the sons of God
the groaning world awaiting redemption
the creation under the curse

schools.summary: Gill (holding 'the creature' the Gentiles) reads the vanity as their idolatry and empty state; Barnes and most read it of the natural creation subjected to vanity (frailty, decay, the curse, Genesis 3:17-18) by God's appointment after the fall, 'not willingly' (not by its own choice) yet 'in hope' of deliverance; the creation subjected to vanity not by its own will but by him (God) who subjected it (the divine sentence after the fall, the creation an innocent sufferer for man's sin, the ground cursed for Adam's sake) — yet 'in hope' (the subjection not final, the hope of restoration, v.21, the curse not the last word).

themes: the creature was made subject to vanity
not willingly, but by reason of him who hath subjected
subjected for man's sin
subjected in hope

schools.summary: Gill (on his Gentile reading) sees the deliverance of the Gentiles from idolatry into gospel liberty; Barnes and most see the natural creation delivered from the bondage of corruption (decay, death, the curse) into a share in the liberty and glory of the children of God at the redemption; the creation delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God — the lifting of the curse (the creation freed from decay and death, the renewal of creation, the new heavens and new earth, 2 Peter 3:13; Revelation 21:1; Acts 3:21), the creation's share in the redemption (its deliverance linked to the manifestation of the sons, v.19, the redemption cosmic).

themes: the creature itself also shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption
into the glorious liberty of the children of God
the renewal of creation
the curse lifted

schools.summary: Barnes takes 'the whole creation' in the natural, usual sense (the creation groaning, as in birth-pangs, longing for the deliverance); Gill reads the groaning of the Gentile world; the whole creation groaning and travailing in pain together until now — the universal groaning likened to the pangs of childbirth (the pain not of death but of a coming birth, the travail issuing in the new creation, the suffering preceding the birth) — the hope in the groaning (the travail toward the deliverance, the pangs pointing to the new creation, not despair but expectation), the long travail 'until now' (from the fall, continuing until the redemption).

themes: the whole creation groaneth and travaileth in pain
together until now
the pangs of a coming birth
the new creation

schools.summary: Barnes: 'the firstfruits of the Spirit' (using the harvest figure) means the first portion, the present graces and influences of the Spirit, the earnest of the full harvest to come; not only the creation but believers also, having the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan within themselves, waiting for the adoption — the redemption of the body (the resurrection and glorification of the body, freed from corruption, like Christ's glorious body, Philippians 3:21); the present adoption (v.15) and the future consummation (the already and the not-yet, the firstfruits now and the harvest then), the firstfruits intensifying the longing (the taste increasing the hunger).

themes: the firstfruits of the Spirit
groan within ourselves, waiting for the adoption
the redemption of our body
the firstfruits now, the full adoption then

schools.summary: Gill: the saints were already saved in the purposes of God, the covenant, and the purchase of Christ, yet the full enjoyment is future, possessed now in hope; we are saved by (or in) hope, for hope that is seen is not hope (hope reaches toward the unseen, the future, the not-yet-possessed) — what a man sees he does not hope for (the object of hope unseen); the present salvation held in the mode of hope (the believer already saved in God's purpose and Christ, yet waiting for the full salvation, the redemption of the body and the glory, the already and the not-yet), the comfort and discipline of hope.

themes: we are saved by hope
hope that is seen is not hope
salvation held in the mode of hope
hope reaches toward the unseen

schools.summary: Barnes: this effect exists everywhere — where there is a strong desire for an object and a corresponding expectation of obtaining it (which constitutes true hope), we can wait for it with patience; if we hope for what we do not see (the future glory, the redemption of the body), we wait for it with patience — the patient endurance that hope produces (the steadfast expectation, the patience of hope sustaining the soul through the present groaning, v.23), the contrast with impatience (desire without expectation frets, but true hope waits), the patience of the assured.

themes: if we hope for that we see not
with patience wait for it
the patient waiting of hope
the soul sustained through groaning

schools.summary: Gill: the Spirit who dwells in us, leads us, witnesses to our adoption, also helps our infirmities — the people of God all have their infirmities, weakness in prayer one; the Spirit helps our infirmities, for we know not what we should pray for as we ought (the ignorance and weakness in prayer), but the Spirit himself makes intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered — the Spirit's inward help (not apart from us but in us, exciting, shaping, and bearing up the believer's prayers, the unutterable groanings too deep for words), supplying our ignorance and weakness, the comfort of the help in prayer.

themes: the Spirit also helpeth our infirmities
we know not what we should pray for
the Spirit maketh intercession... with groanings which cannot be uttered
the Spirit's help in prayer

schools.summary: Barnes: 'he that searcheth the hearts' is God — searching the heart his incommunicable attribute (Jeremiah 17:10; 1 Chronicles 28:9); he knows the mind of the Spirit, the desires the Spirit excites in the heart (the unutterable groanings of v.26 known to God, the wordless prayer read); the Spirit makes intercession for the saints according to the will of God — the certainty of the answer (the Spirit-shaped prayers in line with the divine will sure to be heard, 1 John 5:14), the comfort of the Spirit's interpreting (the inarticulate prayer understood), the harmony of the divine Persons (the Father knowing the interceding Spirit's mind).

themes: he that searcheth the hearts knoweth... the mind of the Spirit
intercession for the saints according to the will of God
the Spirit's groanings understood
the prayer sure to be heard

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul mooted this topic for those in danger — teaching them not to choose just what they may think best but to leave it to God, who works all for good; we know that all things work together for good to them that love God, the called according to his purpose — the providence overruling all things (the pleasant and the painful, even afflictions and sufferings, working together in concert) for the believer's good; the 'good' defined as the true, spiritual, eternal good (conformity to Christ, v.29, final salvation, the glory, not always temporal ease); the persons defined by love to God and the calling according to his purpose.

themes: all things work together for good
to them that love God
the called according to his purpose
the providence overruling all for good

schools.summary: Gill argues the 'foreknowledge' here is not the bare prescience of all things future (for then all would be predestinated and glorified) but a special, distinguishing foreknowledge — God's foreknowing in the sense of foreloving, setting his love on a select people (the argument from the chain, all the foreknown glorified, v.30); whom God foreknew (foreloved) he predestinated to be conformed to the image of his Son (the end of predestination conformity to Christ, in holiness now and glory hereafter), that Christ might be the firstborn among many brethren (the head, pattern, elder brother of the redeemed family); the great debate on 'foreknow' (foreloved vs foresight of who would believe).

debates.question: What does 'whom he did foreknow' mean?

sides.position: Foreknew = foreloved, set his electing love on a select people beforehand (not bare prescience; for if all the foreknown are also glorified, v.30, the foreknowledge must be distinguishing)
God's foresight of who would believe — foreknowing the faith, then predestinating

themes: whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate
conformed to the image of his Son
foreknowledge as electing love
the firstborn among many brethren

schools.summary: Gill: this predestination of particular persons, the effect of divine grace, the source of all the other blessings, placed at the head and securing them all; whom God predestinated he called, whom he called justified, whom he justified glorified — the unbroken golden chain (each link drawing the next, none lost between, the same 'whom... them' throughout), the security of the elect (none predestined is lost, all called, justified, glorified, John 6:39, the perseverance), the certain glorification (spoken of as already done, 'glorified,' so certain it is reckoned accomplished), the comfort of the chain held by God.

themes: whom he did predestinate, them he also called
called... justified... glorified
the unbroken golden chain
the security of the elect

schools.summary: Gill: the question may look to the afflictions (shall we murmur? no) or to the blessings just mentioned (foreknowledge, predestination, calling, justification, glorification) — what can be said to these, a complete set of blessings wanting nothing; if God be for us, who can be against us — the triumphant challenge (the believer, having God himself on his side, the God of the golden chain, vv.29-30, need fear no adversary), the security in God's favour (none can prevail against the one whom the Almighty defends, Psalm 118:6, the adversaries real but powerless), opening the triumphant conclusion of the chapter (vv.31-39).

themes: if God be for us, who can be against us?
what shall we then say to these things?
God on the believer's side
security in the favour of the Almighty

schools.summary: Gill: it is amazing that God should not spare 'his own Son' — his proper Son, of the same nature, equal to him, the Son of his love, who never sinned — when he spared not the angels that sinned nor the old world; he delivered him up for us all; the 'much more' argument from the cross — if God gave the greatest (his own Son, the unsparing gift), how shall he not also freely give us all things (the lesser, all needful for life, salvation, and glory) — the security grounded in the unsparing gift of the Son (the cross the guarantee, the all things given 'with him'/in Christ, 1 Corinthians 3:21-23).

themes: he that spared not his own Son
delivered him up for us all
freely give us all things
security grounded in the greatest gift

schools.summary: Gill: the elect of God a select number whom he has loved and chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world; though chargeable in themselves with sin (Adam's sin, their own sins), none can lay anything to their charge, for it is God that justifies; the challenge — who shall lay anything to the charge of God's elect? It is God that justifieth — the believer secure against all accusation (the highest Judge himself having acquitted them, no charge standing, the sins real but imputed to Christ and not charged), the comfort against the accuser (Satan, conscience, the law silenced, Revelation 12:10).

themes: who shall lay any thing to the charge of God's elect?
it is God that justifieth
none can charge the justified
security against all accusation

schools.summary: Gill: none of God's elect can be condemned, for they are loved with an everlasting love, chosen, in Christ where there is no condemnation, justified by him; who is he that condemneth? — the fourfold ground of security: Christ that died (the satisfaction for sin, the penalty borne), rather is risen again (the proof of the accepted satisfaction, the justification, Romans 4:25), who is at the right hand of God (the exaltation, the authority), who also makes intercession for us (the continual advocacy, Hebrews 7:25; 1 John 2:1) — against which no condemnation can stand, the 'yea rather' emphasizing the living, interceding Christ as the continual security.

themes: who is he that condemneth?
Christ that died, yea rather, that is risen again
at the right hand of God... maketh intercession
the fourfold ground of security

schools.summary: Gill: 'the love of Christ' is not the saints' love to Christ but his love to them (the apostle's design to strengthen the faith and comfort the souls of God's people under their trials); who shall separate us from the love of Christ? — the severest trials named (tribulation, distress, persecution, famine, nakedness, peril, sword, the catalogue of afflictions) unable to sever the believer's union with Christ's everlasting, unchanging love (the very sufferings that seem to threaten it powerless to separate), the comfort under the severest trials (the love that holds through all, the bond the trial cannot break).

themes: who shall separate us from the love of Christ?
tribulation... persecution... peril... sword
the love of Christ to us
the unbreakable union

schools.summary: Gill: cited from Psalm 44:22, the meaning that for the sake of God and his worship the Old Testament saints were frequently put to death or exposed to persecution, as New Testament saints for Christ and his gospel; Barnes: Paul quotes it not as originally referring to Christians but as aptly descriptive of their condition; for God's sake we are killed all the day long (the constant, continual exposure to death, 1 Corinthians 15:31, 'I die daily'), accounted as sheep for the slaughter (helpless victims, appointed to die) — the appointed lot of the people of God to suffer for the truth (2 Timothy 3:12), the sufferings real and severe, yet not separating from the love (v.37).

themes: for thy sake we are killed all the day long
accounted as sheep for the slaughter
the appointed lot of suffering
killed for God's sake

schools.summary: Gill: 'in all these things' — afflictions, distresses, persecutions, famine, nakedness, sword (v.35-36) — the believer is not separated but conquers; Barnes: in the very midst of them, while enduring them, the believer is able to triumph, 'more than' conquerors; the believer more than a conqueror through him that loved us — not merely surviving or escaping the trials but triumphing over them, made the gainer by them (the trials working patience, experience, hope, vv.3-4, and conformity to Christ, turned to victory) — the triumph through Christ's love (1 Corinthians 15:57, the love the power, not the believer's strength).

themes: in all these things we are more than conquerors
through him that loved us
triumphing, made the gainer
the trials turned to victory

schools.summary: Gill: these words express the strong persuasion and full assurance of faith — not conjectural but certain, not from special revelation but founded on the nature of God's love, its security in Christ, eternal predestination, and the unalterable covenant; Paul persuaded that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come can separate us — the full assurance ranging over all conceivable powers and circumstances (death the last enemy, life with its trials, angels good or evil, the spiritual hosts, all time), the certainty grounded in the unchanging love of God (2 Timothy 1:12).

themes: I am persuaded, that neither death, nor life
nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers
the full assurance over all powers
grounded in God's unchanging love

schools.summary: Gill: 'nor height, nor depth' — neither heaven, earth, nor hell, nor any inhabitant of either, no height of honour and prosperity nor depth of adversity, no power or policy in men or devils; 'nor any other creature' — anything else in all creation; nothing shall be able to separate us from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus our Lord — the final, all-comprehending assurance (nothing in all creation can sever the believer, the unbreakable bond, John 10:28, the union eternal), the love of God secured 'in Christ' (conveyed and guaranteed in him, the union with Christ the security), the soaring conclusion crowning the chapter and the great argument (Romans 3-8).

themes: nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature
separate us from the love of God
which is in Christ Jesus our Lord
the unbreakable bond, eternal security
